brief:
The most striking feature of this week's curve is not what moved but what didn't — absolutely nothing. Zero basis points of change across every tenor from 91-day to 20-year, a rare equilibrium that on the surface suggests calm but beneath reveals a market frozen in place, waiting for a catalyst that hasn't yet arrived. The curve itself, however, is far from orderly. The front end prices rationally enough — the 91-day bill at **9.85%** sits just 15 basis points below the repo rate at **10%**, with call money at **9.8%** confirming ample short-term liquidity. But move further out and the anomalies become glaring: the 364-day bill at **10.07%** actually trades **below** the 182-day at **10.2%**, creating an inversion in the belly that signals either a technical distortion from auction timing or a market quietly pricing in rate cuts within twelve months. Then look at the long end, where the 2-year at **11.95%** trades **80 basis points above** the 10-year at **11.15%** and the 15-year at **12.68%** leaps **103 basis points** past the 20-year at **11.65%**. This is not a functioning term structure — it is a curve riddled with kinks born from illiquidity, sporadic issuance, and a secondary market that barely trades. The 10-year auction on March 8 tells the real story: a bid-cover of just **1.4x** and **10% devolvement** on Bangladesh Bank, meaning the market couldn't fully absorb even at **11.15%**. Compare that to the 91-day auction's clean **1.92x** cover and zero devolvement — the market has appetite for duration-free safety, not duration risk.

The structural backdrop explains both the stillness and the distortions. Excess liquidity sits at a commanding **৳1.9 lakh crore**, which ordinarily would compress yields, yet CPI inflation at **9.13%** — with food prices running at **9.3%** — means real returns are negative across most of the curve and deeply negative at the front end. Bangladesh Bank is caught in a familiar bind: the SDF-repo corridor of **7.5% to 11.5%** accommodates the current yield structure, but with inflation still running nearly a full percentage point above the repo rate, there is no room for easing and the market knows it. Fiscal dynamics add another layer of pressure. Revenue collection at just **44.9%** of the FY26 target with the fiscal year half-spent signals a significant borrowing acceleration in Q4, yet ADP implementation at a paltry **11.5%** means the government has been spending almost nothing on development — a pattern that historically reverses sharply in May and June, flooding the market with new supply precisely when banks are scrambling to meet year-end SLR requirements. Total debt at **৳21.5 lakh crore** and an interest-to-revenue ratio of **22%** confirm the fiscal trajectory is tightening, not loosening. Meanwhile, the ADR at **75.8%** gives banks room to lend but credit demand remains subdued enough to keep that excess liquidity parked in the system. FX reserves at **$35.1 billion** and a stable taka at **121.2** remove one potential source of disruption, and Brent crude at **$78.4** alongside LNG at **$12.8/mmbtu** are manageable — no energy price shock is on the horizon to destabilize the current account.

Here is the call for this week. The front end — 91-day and 364-day bills — is fully priced and offers no value beyond parking; with real yields negative by 70-plus basis points after inflation, we should hold only what SLR demands and not a taka more. The opportunity sits in the **5-year segment at 10.82%**, which cleared cleanly at **1.6x** cover with zero devolvement and offers a **real yield approaching 170 basis points** over current CPI — the most attractive risk-adjusted carry on the curve. I would recommend building our 5-year position methodically over the next two to three auctions before the fiscal year-end supply deluge compresses bid-cover ratios and forces yields higher. Avoid the 2-year entirely — at **11.95%** it is an illiquidity premium masquerading as yield, and any normalization of the curve will see that tenor reprice violently tighter. The 10-year requires caution; that **10% devolvement** is a warning flag, and I would not add duration beyond five years until we see bid-covers above **1.8x** in the long bond. The single most important catalyst to watch is the April T-bill auction cycle and any signal from Bangladesh Bank on Q4 government borrowing calendars. If the Ministry of Finance front-loads its borrowing to close the revenue gap, we could see 50-75 basis points of upward pressure across the 5-to-10-year segment — making today's 5-year at **10.82%** look like a gift in hindsight.
